name: DuckDB
summary: Raasveldt-Mühleisen CIDR 2020: a modern analytical database that explicitly rejects JIT compilation. Vectorised interpreter with tightly-tuned SIMD kernels reaches competitive throughput without compilation latency. The design point: JIT compile time is lethal for interactive analytics where queries run in milliseconds.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
**DuckDB** (Raasveldt & Mühleisen, CIDR 2020) is a notable outlier: a modern analytical database that explicitly rejects JIT compilation. Instead, DuckDB uses a vectorised interpreter with tightly-tuned SIMD kernels, similar to Photon's approach.

The authors' argument: JIT compilation latency is lethal for interactive analytics where queries run in milliseconds; a 50ms compile time means 50ms of user-observed wait even if the query itself runs in 5ms. Vectorised interpretation reaches competitive throughput without any compilation phase at all.

sections.title: Design rationale
sections.content:
DuckDB's position is a useful counterexample to the "compile everything" orthodoxy. When query latency matters more than throughput, and when the query plan space is small enough to pre-tune kernels, interpretation beats JIT. The design choice depends on workload.

Production usage of DuckDB has expanded substantially: data scientists embed it in Jupyter for ad-hoc analysis; backend services use it as a local analytical engine; the design has spawned imitators (Apache Doris, ClickHouse partial-vectorisation paths). DuckDB's existence and adoption demonstrate that the vectorised-no-JIT design point is robust at production scale.
